steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims obedience must be ‘as close to absolute as possible’ and that ‘even minor infractions should not be ignored.’ The question asks which quote best supports this claim. The needed evidence must show why small violations are dangerous — specifically, how they accumulate and ultimately threaten the state.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "When contrasting different forms of government, Aristotle holds that "oligarchies may last, not from any inherent stability in such forms of government, but because the rulers are on good terms both with the unenfranchised and with the governing classes." That is, oligarchic leaders who wish to hold on to power will introduce members of disenfranchised classes into government in a participatory role.": Addresses how rulers hold power, not how minor violations accumulate into larger threats. ✗
  • ""When constructing his argument regarding the characteristics of a well-functioning government, Aristotle asserts that 'Transgression creeps in unperceived and at last ruins the state,' illustrating this idea with a comparison to frequent small expenditures slowly and almost imperceptibly chipping away at a fortune until it is ultimately depleted."": Directly shows the mechanism: small infractions go unnoticed, build silently, and eventually cause ruin — exactly why minor violations cannot be tolerated. ✓
  • ""When Aristotle considers the health of constitutions, he states that 'Constitutions are preserved when their destroyers are at a distance, and sometimes also because they are near, for the fear of them makes the government keep in hand the constitution.' He holds that rulers who wish to see constitutions preserved must continually remind the populace of the dangers that would result from a constitutional collapse."": Explains why people obey out of fear, not why small infractions are cumulatively dangerous. ✗
  • ""When Aristotle writes on the necessity of avoiding corruption in government, he proposes that 'every state should be so administered and so regulated by law that its magistrates cannot possibly make money, in particular, he thinks oligarchies are particularly susceptible to corruption through bribery."": Discusses corruption generally, not the specific accumulation of minor violations into catastrophic harm. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
